1	## Bash

12	```
13	{"additions":81,"body":"## Summary\n\n- Replaces the daily cron + LLM-routed orchestrator with a deterministic, per-ticket router\n- Triggered by Jira automation on the \"Dev Ready\" status transition via `repository_dispatch` (`event_type: jira-dev-ready`)\n- Pure SP-based routing: `SP ≤ 3 → junior-dev.yml`, `SP ≥ 4 → senior-dev.yml` (phase: `propose`); unpointed tickets fail the workflow loudly\n- Removes `agents/orchestrator/{README,prompt}.md` — no model in the routing path means no prompt to maintain\n- README/doc updates reflect the new trigger surface and the runtime split (plain bash for the orchestrator, `claude-code-action` for read/triage agents, raw `claude -p` for implementation lanes)\n\n## Why\n\nThe cron was adding up to ~24h of latency per ticket and burning Claude budget on what is fundamentally an `if/else`. Event-driven dispatch is per-ticket, deterministic, and removes the LLM from the routing path entirely.\n\n## Jira-side setup (one-time, not in code)\n\nDocumented in the workflow header. Needs a fine-grained GitHub PAT (`Contents: read`, `Actions: read/write`) stored as a Jira automation credential, plus a rule on the Dev Ready transition that POSTs to `https://api.github.com/repos/<owner>/<repo>/dispatches` with `{\"event_type\":\"jira-dev-ready\",\"client_payload\":{\"ticket_key\":\"{{issue.key}}\"}}`.\n\nAlready configured and confirmed end-to-end in Jira's audit log (rule fires → web request published successfully). Won't reach the new workflow until this PR merges, since `repository_dispatch` only fires workflows on the default branch.\n\n## Test plan\n\n- [ ] Merge to `main` so `repository_dispatch` can match the new trigger\n- [ ] Move a real ticket with story points to \"Development Ready\"; verify orchestrator run appears in Actions and dispatches the correct dev workflow (junior for SP≤3, senior for SP≥4)\n- [ ] Move a ticket with no story points; verify orchestrator fails loudly with `has no story points`\n- [ ] Confirm the manual 
14	```

28	+#   SP <= 3  -> junior-dev.yml

30	+#   no SP    -> workflow fails loudly; no downstream dispatch
31	+#

33	+# ---------------

35	+# workflow_dispatch is kept for manual recovery and dry-runs from the
36	+# Actions UI.

159	{"path":".github/workflows/orchestrator.yml","line":109,"body":"**Severity: Medium**\n\nOperation ordering: `gh workflow run` fires the dev workflow before `jira issue move` transitions the ticket. If the Jira call fails (API outage, permission error, wrong status), `set -euo pipefail` correctly aborts the job — but the dev workflow has already been dispatched. The ticket stays in \"Dev Ready\" with a live workflow run it doesn't know about.\n\nReversing the order is safer:\n- Transition fails → job fails, ticket stays in Dev Ready, no dispatch happened → clean recovery (fix and re-trigger).\n- Transition succeeds, dispatch fails → ticket is In Progress with no dev work started → human moves it back to Dev Ready to re-trigger.\n\n```suggestion\n          jira issue move \"$TICKET_KEY\" \"In Progress\"\n          gh workflow run \"${target}.yml\" -f ticket_key=\"$TICKET_KEY\"\n```","confirmed":true}
160	```

[[ \"$sp\" =~ ^[0-9]+(\\.[0-9]+)?$ ]]; then\n            echo \"::error::${TICKET_KEY} story points '${sp}' is not a number; refusing to route. Check customfield_10016.\"\n            exit 1\n          fi\n          sp_int=$(printf '%.0f' \"$sp\")\n```","confirmed":true}

## Bash

gh pr view 50 --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName
```

## Bash
```
gh pr diff 50
```

## Result
```
{"additions":81,"body":"## Summary\n\n- Replaces the daily cron + LLM-routed orchestrator with a deterministic, per-ticket router\n- Triggered by Jira automation on the \"Dev Ready\" status transition via `repository_dispatch` (`event_type: jira-dev-ready`)\n- Pure SP-based routing: `SP ≤ 3 → junior-dev.yml`, `SP ≥ 4 → senior-dev.yml` (phase: `propose`); unpointed tickets fail the workflow loudly\n- Removes `agents/orchestrator/{README,prompt}.md` — no model in the routing path means no prompt to maintain\n- README/doc updates reflect the new trigger surface and the runtime split (plain bash for the orchestrator, `claude-code-action` for read/triage agents, raw `claude -p` for implementation lanes)\n\n## Why\n\nThe cron was adding up to ~24h of latency per ticket and burning Claude budget on what is fundamentally an `if/else`. Event-driven dispatch is per-ticket, deterministic, and removes the LLM from the routing path entirely.\n\n## Jira-side setup (one-time, not in code)\n\nDocumented in the workflow header. Needs a fine-grained GitHub PAT (`Contents: read`, `Actions: read/write`) stored as a Jira automation credential, plus a rule on the Dev Ready transition that POSTs to `https://api.github.com/repos/<owner>/<repo>/dispatches` with `{\"event_type\":\"jira-dev-ready\",\"client_payload\":{\"ticket_key\":\"{{issue.key}}\"}}`.\n\nAlready configured and confirmed end-to-end in Jira's audit log (rule fires → web request published successfully). Won't reach the new workflow until this PR merges, since `repository_dispatch` only fires workflows on the default branch.\n\n## Test plan\n\n- [ ] Merge to `main` so `repository_dispatch` can match the new trigger\n- [ ] Move a real ticket with story points to \"Development Ready\"; verify orchestrator run appears in Actions and dispatches the correct dev workflow (junior for SP≤3, senior for SP≥4)\n- [ ] Move a ticket with no story points; verify orchestrator fails loudly with `has no story points`\n- [ ] Confirm the manual 
```

## Result
```
diff --git a/.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml b/.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml
index f4d6bc4..b64bc79 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml
+++ b/.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml
@@ -1,16 +1,51 @@
-name: Development Orchestrator agent
+# Orchestrator (Jira Dev Ready)
+#
+# Deterministic router from Jira to the dev agents. Fires per-ticket on the
+# "Dev Ready" status transition and dispatches by story points:
+#   SP <= 3  -> junior-dev.yml
+#   SP >= 4  -> senior-dev.yml (default phase: propose)
+#   no SP    -> workflow fails loudly; no downstream dispatch
+#
+# Trigger surface
+# ---------------
+# repository_dispatch (event_type: jira-dev-ready) is the production path.
+# workflow_dispatch is kept for manual recovery and dry-runs from the
+# Actions UI.
+#
+# Jira-side setup (one-time, configured in Jira automation, not in code)
+# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
+# 1. Create a fine-grained GitHub PAT with `Contents: read` and
+#    `Actions: read and write` on this repo. Store as a Jira automation
+#    secret (e.g. GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT).
+# 2. Add a Jira automation rule:
+#    - Trigger: Issue transitioned -> To status: "Dev Ready"
+#    - Action:  Send web request
+#        URL:    https://api.github.com/repos/<owner>/<repo>/dispatches
+#        Method: POST
+#        Headers:
+#          Authorization: Bearer <PAT>
+#          Accept: application/vnd.github+json
+#          Content-Type: application/json
+#        Body:
+#          {"event_type":"jira-dev-ready","client_payload":{"ticket_key":"{{issue.key}}"}}
+
+name: Orchestrator (Jira Dev Ready)
 
 on:
-  schedule:
-    - cron: '47 7 * * *'
+  repository_dispatch:
+    types: [jira-dev-ready]
   workflow_dispatch:
+    inputs:
+      ticket_key:
+        description: 'Jira ticket key (e.g. KAN-42)'
+        required: true
+        type: string
 
 jobs:
   route:
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     permissions:
-      contents: write
-      id-token: wri
```

content.thinking: Now let me read the agent prompt and workflow files to understand the full picture.

content.text: Now let me read the agent prompt and workflow.

content.content:
1	You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
2	checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
3	and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.
4	
5	## Your job
6	
7	Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
8	Nothing else.
9	
10	## What to look for
11	
12	Focus on these four buckets, in this order:
13	
14	1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
15	   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
16	2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
17	   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
18	   boundaries.
19	3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
20	   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
21	4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
22	   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.
23	
24	Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
25	musings, hypothetical future requirements.
26	
27	## How to gather context
28	
29	- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
30	- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input. Don't use
31	  `git diff` — the checkout is shallow (`fetch-depth: 1`) so comparisons
32	  against main have no merge base and will fail.
33	- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
34	  changed lines.
35	
36	### Verifying external facts
37	
38	Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
39	library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
40	Look it up before flagging it.
41	
42	- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
43	  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
44	  Prefer this over web search for docs.
45	- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
46	  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
47	  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
48	  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.
49	
50	If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
51	question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.
52	
53	## How to post feedback
54	
55	### Inline comments (primary)
56	
57	Specific issues go in inline comments via
58	`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
59	One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
60	comment must include:
61	
62	- A one-line description of the issue.
63	- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
64	- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
65	  change. Be concrete; reference the code.
66	
67	Severity rubric:
68	- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
69	- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
70	- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
71	- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.
72	
73	### High-level overview (optional)
74	
75	If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
76	else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
77	via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
78	sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
79	have nothing to add at that level. Escape any backticks in the `--body` string as `\`` to avoid shell interpretation.
80	
81	If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
82	"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.
83	
84	## When you're done
85	
86	Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner will exit.
87

file.content:
You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.

## Your job

Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
Nothing else.

## What to look for

Focus on these four buckets, in this order:

1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
   boundaries.
3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.

Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
musings, hypothetical future requirements.

## How to gather context

- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input. Don't use
  `git diff` — the checkout is shallow (`fetch-depth: 1`) so comparisons
  against main have no merge base and will fail.
- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
  changed lines.

### Verifying external facts

Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
Look it up before flagging it.

- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
  Prefer this over web search for docs.
- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.

If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.

## How to post feedback

### Inline comments (primary)

Specific issues go in inline comments via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
comment must include:

- A one-line description of the issue.
- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
  change. Be concrete; reference the code.

Severity rubric:
- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.

### High-level overview (optional)

If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
have nothing to add at that level. Escape any backticks in the `--body` string as `\`` to avoid shell interpretation.

If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.

## When you're done

Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner will exit.

file.content:
name: PR Review agent

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, ready_for_review, reopened]
    paths-ignore:
      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
      checks: read
      statuses: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - id: prompt
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
            echo
            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          track_progress: false
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"

      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin "$BRANCH"

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
            -f agent_name=pr-review \
            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"

content.thinking:
Now let me analyze the run summary vs the current prompt and workflow to look for concrete issues:

**Run summary analysis:**

The run went like this:
1. `gh pr view 50 --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — success
2. `gh pr diff 50` — success  
3. Assistant said "Let me look at the current orchestrator file and load the inline comment tool"
4. ToolSearch for `mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment`
5. Read of `/home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml`
6. Two inline comments were posted successfully

Looking at what happened, the agent searched for the inline comment tool even though it was already in the allowed tools list, which was unnecessary. The assistant also narrated its intentions before executing, which the prompt discourages.

Reading the full orchestrator file made sense since the diff alone wouldn't provide the line numbers needed for precise inline comments. The agent didn't post a high-level overview comment, but that's optional per the instructions. The ToolSearch call before using the inline comment tool was redundant since the tool was already available. The ToolSearch call looks unnecessary since the MCP tool was already in the allowedTools list and the system prompt explicitly told the agent to use it. The agent could have called it directly without the extra search step.

Wait, but I'm seeing a system reminder that deferred tools need to be loaded via ToolSearch first — their schemas aren't available until you search for them. So the agent was actually following the correct pattern by using ToolSearch before calling the tool. The result from ToolSearch is a bit odd though, just returning a tool reference rather than the full schema.

Looking at the prompt itself, it mentions the inline comment tool but doesn't explicitly instruct the agent to use ToolSearch first. The agent figured this out through trial and error, which means there's an opportunity to make the prompt clearer upfront so agents don't waste a round-trip discovering this requirement. The run was actually pretty efficient overall—the agent gathered the necessary context, read the file to get exact line numbers, and posted two well-reasoned inline comments. I'm noticing the agent had already seen the full diff earlier, so reading the orchestrator file was specifically to map the diff's relative line numbers to the actual file positions for the comments.

Looking back at the ToolSearch call for the inline comment tool, it was necessary since the system requires deferred MCP tools to go through ToolSearch first. Though I'm wondering if the prompt should explicitly mention this requirement to save the agent from having to figure it out. The prompt could explicitly instruct the agent to call ToolSearch before using the inline comment tool for the first time, which would save a tool call per run, though this is a minor optimization since the system reminder already guides the agent correctly. The real constraint is that MCP tool schemas can't be preloaded in the workflow except through the claudeAction itself, so there's limited room to improve efficiency here.

Looking at the actual run, it was quite clean—two data gathering calls, one necessary ToolSearch, one Read for line numbers, and two successful inline comments with no retries or redundant exploration. The agent stopped after completing the task as instructed, so there's nothing concrete to optimize.

Though I'm wondering if the prompt could suggest batching the ToolSearch with the initial GitHub calls to run them in parallel from the start, rather than treating it as a separate step. But the ToolSearch is architecturally required to load the MCP schema, so there's no real way around it. before the agent actually ran those commands. So the agent knew it would need the inline comment tool upfront. That means ToolSearch could genuinely run in parallel with the initial data gathering. But this is such a minor optimization—saving one sequential step—that it's not worth a prompt change. The real bottleneck is the Read call to get line numbers, which legitimately needs to happen after seeing the diff. The workflow was efficient given the constraints, so there's nothing concrete to fix here.

The agent stopped immediately after the last tool call with no trailing narration. No failed-then-retried commands, no redundant exploration, no wandering scope.

Nothing concrete to suggest from this run.
